chapter.title: Chemical Bonding and Molecular Structure
chapter.summary:
This chapter, "Chemical Bonding and Molecular Structure," explores the fundamental principles governing how atoms combine to form molecules. It begins with the Kössel-Lewis approach, introducing ionic and covalent bonding based on the octet rule and Lewis structures, emphasizing valence electrons and their role in bonding. Formal charge is introduced to aid in selecting the most stable Lewis structure. Limitations of the octet rule are also discussed.

The chapter then delves into the energetics of ionic bond formation, defining lattice enthalpy and its significance. Bond parameters such as bond length, bond angle, and bond enthalpy are described, along with the concept of bond order and resonance. The polarity of bonds and dipole moments are introduced to quantify unequal sharing of electrons. Fajans' rules explain the partial covalent character in ionic bonds.

VSEPR theory is presented as a tool for predicting molecular shapes based on electron pair repulsions. Valence Bond (VB) theory explains covalent bonding through atomic orbital overlap, while the concept of hybridization (sp, sp2, sp3, and with d orbitals) refines VB theory to explain molecular geometries. Finally, Molecular Orbital (MO) theory describes bonding by combining atomic orbitals to form molecular orbitals, leading to bond order calculations and predictions about molecular stability and magnetic properties. The chapter concludes with an explanation of hydrogen bonding and its types. Each section builds upon the previous, culminating in a comprehensive understanding of chemical bonding principles and their applications. This knowledge is crucial for predicting molecular properties and understanding chemical reactions.

subsections.title: Introduction to Chemical Bonding
subsections.content_text: Matter is composed of elements. Under normal conditions, noble gases are the only elements that exist as independent atoms in nature. Other atoms group together as molecules, which are species with characteristic properties. The attractive force which holds various constituents (atoms, ions, etc.) together in different chemical species is called a chemical bond. Atoms combine in various ways to form chemical compounds, raising questions about why they combine and why only certain combinations are possible. Every system tends toward stability, and bonding is nature’s way of lowering the energy of the system to attain this state. Key theories developed to explain these phenomena include the Kössel-Lewis approach, Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion (VSEPR) Theory, Valence Bond (VB) Theory, and Molecular Orbital (MO) Theory.

subsections.title: Lewis's Atomic Model and the Octet
subsections.content_text: In 1916, G.N. Lewis and Walther Kössel independently provided explanations for valence based on the inertness of noble gases. Lewis pictured the atom as a positively charged 'Kernel' (consisting of the nucleus and inner electrons) surrounded by an outer shell that could accommodate a maximum of eight electrons. He envisioned these eight electrons occupying the corners of a cube around the Kernel. For instance, the single outer shell electron of sodium would occupy one corner, while in noble gases, all eight corners are occupied, representing a particularly stable electronic arrangement. Lewis postulated that atoms achieve the stable octet when they are linked by chemical bonds. This can occur through the transfer of electrons (as in the formation of $Na^+$ and $Cl^-$) or by the sharing of electron pairs (as in $Cl_2$, $H_2$, and $F_2$).

subsections.title: Lewis Symbols and Valence Electrons
subsections.content_text: During the formation of a molecule, only the outer shell electrons, known as valence electrons, participate in chemical combination. The inner shell electrons are well-protected and generally do not participate. G.N. Lewis introduced simple notations to represent these valence electrons, known as Lewis symbols. For example, the Lewis symbols for the elements of the second period are represented by the element's symbol surrounded by a number of dots equal to its valence electrons.

diagrams.label: Lewis Symbols
diagrams.description: Lewis symbols for second-period elements: Li (one dot), Be (two dots), B (three dots), C (four dots), N (five dots), O (six dots), F (seven dots), and Ne (eight dots).

subsections.title: Significance of Lewis Symbols and Group Valence
subsections.content_text: The number of dots in a Lewis symbol represents the number of valence electrons in the atom. This count is used to calculate the common or group valence of the element. The group valence is generally either equal to the number of dots in the Lewis symbol or equal to $8$ minus the number of dots.

subsections.title: Kössel's Postulates on Ionic Bonding
subsections.content_text: Kössel's approach to chemical bonding focused on several key facts: 1) Highly electronegative halogens and highly electropositive alkali metals are separated in the periodic table by noble gases. 2) The formation of a negative ion from a halogen and a positive ion from an alkali metal involves the gain and loss of an electron, respectively. 3) The resulting ions attain stable noble gas configurations ($ns^2 np^6$, except for Helium which has a duet). 4) These negative and positive ions are stabilized by electrostatic attraction.

subsections.title: Formation of Ionic Compounds: NaCl and CaF2
subsections.content_text: The formation of $NaCl$ from sodium and chlorine is explained by electron transfer: $Na \rightarrow Na^+ + e^-$ ($[Ne] 3s^1 \rightarrow [Ne]$); $Cl + e^- \rightarrow Cl^-$ ($[Ne] 3s^2 3p^5 \rightarrow [Ne] 3s^2 3p^6$ or $[Ar]$); $Na^+ + Cl^- \rightarrow NaCl$ or $Na^+Cl^-$. Similarly, the formation of $CaF_2$ involves: $Ca \rightarrow Ca^{2+} + 2e^-$ ($[Ar] 4s^2 \rightarrow [Ar]$); $F + e^- \rightarrow F^-$ ($[He] 2s^2 2p^5 \rightarrow [He] 2s^2 2p^6$ or $[Ne]$); $Ca^{2+} + 2F^- \rightarrow CaF_2$ or $Ca^{2+}(F^-)_2$.

subsections.title: Electrovalent Bond and Electrovalence
subsections.content_text: The bond formed as a result of the electrostatic attraction between positive and negative ions is termed an electrovalent bond. The electrovalence is equal to the number of unit charges on the ion. For example, calcium has a positive electrovalence of two ($+2$), while chlorine has a negative electrovalence of one ($-1$). Kössel's postulates are fundamental to understanding the formation of ionic crystalline compounds.

subsections.title: Electronic Theory of Chemical Bonding
subsections.content_text: The electronic theory of chemical bonding, developed by Kössel and Lewis in 1916, states that atoms combine either by the transfer of valence electrons (gaining or losing) or by the sharing of valence electrons to achieve an octet in their valence shells. This principle is known as the octet rule.

subsections.title: Lewis-Langmuir Theory and the Chlorine Molecule
subsections.content_text: In 1919, Langmuir refined Lewis's postulates by introducing the term covalent bond and moving away from the stationary cubical arrangement model. The theory is illustrated by the formation of the chlorine molecule, $Cl_2$. A chlorine atom ($[Ne] 3s^2 3p^5$) needs one electron to reach the argon configuration. In $Cl_2$, two chlorine atoms share one pair of electrons, with each atom contributing one electron to the pair. This sharing allows both atoms to attain a stable octet.

diagrams.label: Covalent bond in Cl2
diagrams.description: Lewis dot structure showing two chlorine atoms sharing a single pair of electrons, represented by two dots between the symbols, with each atom surrounded by six additional non-bonding electrons to complete their octets.

subsections.title: Lewis Dot Structures for Simple Molecules
subsections.content_text: Lewis dot structures represent molecules using dots for electrons. Key conditions for these structures include: 1) Each bond results from sharing an electron pair. 2) Each combining atom contributes at least one electron to the shared pair. 3) Atoms attain noble gas configurations through sharing. In water ($H_2O$), the oxygen atom shares electrons with two hydrogen atoms, allowing hydrogen to attain a duplet and oxygen an octet. In carbon tetrachloride ($CCl_4$), the central carbon atom shares electron pairs with four chlorine atoms, resulting in octets for all five atoms.

diagrams.label: Lewis Dot Structures for H2O and CCl4
diagrams.description: Diagrams showing the sharing of electron pairs in H2O (O sharing with two H) and CCl4 (C sharing with four Cl), with circles drawn around each atom to indicate the attainment of duplets or octets.

subsections.title: Multiple Bonds: Double Bonds
subsections.content_text: When two atoms share one electron pair, they form a single covalent bond. Multiple bonds occur when atoms share more than one pair. If two atoms share two pairs of electrons, the bond is called a double bond. For example, in carbon dioxide ($CO_2$), the central carbon atom forms double bonds with two oxygen atoms ($O=C=O$). Similarly, in the ethene molecule ($C_2H_4$), the two carbon atoms are joined by a double bond. When combining atoms share three electron pairs, a triple bond is formed. This is observed in the nitrogen molecule ($N_2$) and the ethyne molecule ($C_2H_2$). In the $N_2$ molecule, each nitrogen atom (valence configuration $2s^2 2p^3$) contributes three electrons to form three shared pairs, allowing both atoms to satisfy the octet rule. In the ethyne molecule ($C_2H_2$), the two carbon atoms are joined by a triple bond, and each carbon atom is also bonded to a hydrogen atom by a single bond ($H-C\equiv C-H$).

diagrams.label: Double bonds in CO2
diagrams.description: Lewis dot structure of CO2 showing the carbon atom sharing four electrons (two pairs) with each oxygen atom, and each oxygen atom retaining two lone pairs to complete its octet.

diagrams.label: C2H4 molecule
diagrams.description: Lewis dot and dash structures of ethene showing a double bond between carbon atoms.

diagrams.label: N2 molecule
diagrams.description: Lewis dot and dash structures of nitrogen showing a triple bond between nitrogen atoms.

diagrams.label: C2H2 molecule
diagrams.description: Lewis dot and dash structures of ethyne showing a triple bond between carbon atoms.

sections.title: Lewis Representation of Simple Molecules (the Lewis Structures)

subsections.title: Introduction to Lewis Structures
subsections.content_text: Lewis dot structures provide a picture of bonding in molecules and ions in terms of shared pairs of electrons and the octet rule. While such a picture may not explain the bonding and behaviour of a molecule completely, it helps significantly in understanding the formation and properties of molecules. Writing Lewis dot structures is a systematic process involving the distribution of valence electrons to satisfy the octet rule for all constituent atoms.

subsections.title: Step 1: Total Valence Electrons
subsections.content_text: The total number of electrons required for writing the structures is obtained by adding the valence electrons of the combining atoms. For example, in the $CH_4$ molecule, there are eight valence electrons available for bonding: 4 from the carbon atom ($2s^2 2p^2$) and 4 from the four hydrogen atoms ($1s^1$).

subsections.title: Step 2: Accounting for Ionic Charges
subsections.content_text: For anions, each negative charge indicates the addition of one electron to the total number of valence electrons. For example, for the $CO_3^{2-}$ ion, two additional electrons are added. For cations, each positive charge indicates the subtraction of one electron from the total. For the $NH_4^+$ ion, one electron is subtracted from the group of neutral atoms.

subsections.title: Step 3: Skeletal Structure and Central Atom
subsections.content_text: Knowing the chemical symbols and the skeletal structure (known or guessed intelligently), the total electrons are distributed as bonding shared pairs. In general, the least electronegative atom occupies the central position. For example, in $NF_3$ and $CO_3^{2-}$, nitrogen and carbon are the central atoms, while fluorine and oxygen occupy terminal positions. Hydrogen and fluorine usually occupy terminal positions.

subsections.title: Step 4: Electron Distribution and Octet Rule
subsections.content_text: After accounting for shared pairs for single bonds, the remaining electron pairs are utilized for multiple bonding or remain as lone pairs. The basic requirement is that each bonded atom gets an octet of electrons. Hydrogen is an exception, as it attains the configuration of helium (a duplet of electrons).

subsections.title: Lewis Representations of Common Molecules
subsections.content_text: Lewis representations for several molecules and ions are provided in Table 4.1, illustrating the application of the octet rule and the distribution of bonding and lone pairs.

subsections.title: Worked Example: Lewis Structure of Carbon Monoxide (CO)
subsections.content_text: To write the Lewis structure of $CO$: 1. Total valence electrons = $4 + 6 = 10$. 2. Skeletal structure: C O. 3. A single bond $:C : \ddot{O}:$ leaves carbon with only 4 electrons. 4. To satisfy the octet for carbon, a triple bond is formed by sharing two more pairs from oxygen: $:C \equiv O:$.

worked_examples.label: Problem 4.1
worked_examples.problem: Write the Lewis dot structure of CO molecule.
worked_examples.solution: Step 1. Count total valence electrons: $4 + 6 = 10$. Step 2. Skeletal structure: C O. Step 3. Single bond $:C : \ddot{O}:$ or $:C - \ddot{O}:$ does not complete the octet on carbon. Step 4. Resort to triple bond: $:C \equiv O:$. This satisfies the octet rule for both atoms.

subsections.title: Worked Example: Lewis Structure of Nitrite Ion (NO2-)
subsections.content_text: To write the Lewis structure of $NO_2^-$: 1. Total valence electrons = $5 + (2 \times 6) + 1 = 18$. 2. Skeletal structure: O N O. 3. Single bonds $[\ddot{O}-\ddot{N}-\ddot{O}]^-$ leave nitrogen with only 6 electrons. 4. Form a double bond with one oxygen to complete the nitrogen octet: $[\ddot{O}=\ddot{N}-\ddot{O}]^-$.

worked_examples.label: Problem 4.2
worked_examples.problem: Write the Lewis structure of the nitrite ion, $NO_2^-$.
worked_examples.solution: Step 1. Total valence electrons = $5 + 12 + 1 = 18$. Step 2. Skeletal structure: O N O. Step 3. Single bonds $[\ddot{O}-\ddot{N}-\ddot{O}]^-$ leave N with 6 electrons. Step 4. Form double bond: $[\ddot{O}=\ddot{N}-\ddot{O}]^-$ or $[\ddot{O}-\ddot{N}=\ddot{O}]^-$.

subsections.title: Definition and Mathematical Expression
subsections.content_text: Formal charge (F.C.) is assigned to atoms in a Lewis structure to track valence electrons. It is defined as the difference between the number of valence electrons in the free atom and the number of electrons assigned to it in the Lewis structure: $F.C. = [V] - [L] - \frac{1}{2}[S]$, where $V$ is the total number of valence electrons in the free atom, $L$ is the total number of non-bonding (lone pair) electrons, and $S$ is the total number of bonding (shared) electrons.

subsections.title: Calculation Example: Ozone (O3)
subsections.content_text: In the ozone molecule ($O_3$), atoms are numbered 1 (central), 2 (double-bonded), and 3 (single-bonded). 1. Central O (1): $F.C. = 6 - 2 - \frac{1}{2}(6) = +1$. 2. Double-bonded O (2): $F.C. = 6 - 4 - \frac{1}{2}(4) = 0$. 3. Single-bonded O (3): $F.C. = 6 - 6 - \frac{1}{2}(2) = -1$. The structure is represented as $O_3$ with these charges.

diagrams.label: O3 Formal Charge
diagrams.description: Lewis structure of ozone with atoms numbered 1, 2, and 3 showing formal charges of +1, 0, and -1 respectively.

subsections.title: Significance of Formal Charge
subsections.content_text: Formal charges help in selecting the lowest energy structure from multiple possible Lewis structures. Generally, the structure with the smallest formal charges is the most stable. It is based on a pure covalent view where electrons are shared equally.

subsections.title: General Overview
subsections.content_text: The octet rule is not universal and has three main types of exceptions: incomplete octets, odd-electron molecules, and expanded octets.

subsections.title: The Incomplete Octet of the Central Atom
subsections.content_text: In some compounds, the central atom has fewer than eight electrons. This occurs in elements with fewer than four valence electrons, such as $Li$ (in $LiCl$), $Be$ (in $BeH_2$), and $B$ (in $BCl_3$). Other examples include $AlCl_3$ and $BF_3$.

diagrams.label: Incomplete Octet Examples
diagrams.description: Lewis structures of LiCl, BeH2, and BCl3 showing central atoms with 2, 4, and 6 electrons respectively.

subsections.title: Odd-electron Molecules
subsections.content_text: Molecules with an odd number of electrons, like nitric oxide ($NO$) and nitrogen dioxide ($NO_2$), cannot satisfy the octet rule for all atoms.

diagrams.label: Odd-electron Molecules
diagrams.description: Lewis structures of NO and NO2 showing unpaired electrons on nitrogen.

subsections.title: The Expanded Octet
subsections.content_text: Elements in and beyond the third period have $3d$ orbitals available for bonding. In compounds like $PF_5$, $SF_6$, and $H_2SO_4$, the central atom has more than eight valence electrons (10, 12, and 12 respectively). This is known as the expanded octet.

diagrams.label: Expanded Octet Examples
diagrams.description: Lewis structures of PF5 (10e), SF6 (12e), and H2SO4 (12e) showing expanded octets.

subsections.title: Other Drawbacks of the Octet Theory
subsections.content_text: While the octet rule is a useful guide, it has several significant limitations. Firstly, sulphur forms compounds like sulphur dichloride ($SCl_2$) where the octet rule is obeyed, but it also forms expanded octet compounds. Secondly, the rule is based on the chemical inertness of noble gases; however, some noble gases like xenon and krypton react with oxygen and fluorine to form compounds such as $XeF_2$, $KrF_2$, and $XeOF_2$. Thirdly, the theory does not account for the three-dimensional shapes of molecules. Finally, it fails to explain the relative stability of molecules, as it provides no information about the energy of a molecule.

diagrams.label: Lewis structure of SCl2
diagrams.description: Lewis dot structure of sulphur dichloride showing the central sulphur atom with two lone pairs and two single bonds to chlorine atoms, each chlorine having three lone pairs, satisfying the octet for all atoms.

subsections.title: Formation of Ionic Bonds
subsections.content_text: The formation of an ionic bond depends primarily on two factors: (1) The ease of formation of positive and negative ions from neutral atoms, and (2) The arrangement of these ions in a solid lattice. Ionic bonds are formed most easily between elements with low ionization enthalpies (typically metals) and elements with high negative electron gain enthalpies (typically non-metals). While most cations are metallic, the ammonium ion ($NH_4^+$) is a notable exception as it is composed of non-metallic elements but forms the cation in many ionic compounds.

subsections.title: Energy Considerations in Ionization and Electron Gain
subsections.content_text: The formation of a positive ion involves ionization (removal of electrons), which is always an endothermic process: $M(g) \rightarrow M^+(g) + e^-$. The formation of a negative ion involves the addition of electrons, characterized by the electron gain enthalpy ($\Delta_{eg}H$): $X(g) + e^- \rightarrow X^-(g)$. This process can be exothermic or endothermic. For example, the formation of $Na^+(g)$ requires $495.8 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$, while the electron gain for $Cl(g)$ releases $-348.7 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$. The net energy difference ($147.1 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$) is compensated by the energy released during lattice formation.

subsections.title: Structure of Ionic Solids
subsections.content_text: Ionic compounds in the crystalline state consist of orderly three-dimensional arrangements of cations and anions held together by coulombic interaction energies. These compounds crystallize in different structures determined by ion size, packing arrangements, and other factors. For instance, sodium chloride ($NaCl$) adopts the rock salt structure. The stability of an ionic compound is qualitatively measured by its enthalpy of lattice formation rather than just achieving an octet of electrons.

diagrams.label: Rock salt structure
diagrams.description: A 3D cubic lattice model of Sodium Chloride (NaCl) showing the alternating arrangement of $Na^+$ (smaller blue spheres) and $Cl^-$ (larger red spheres) ions.

subsections.title: Definition and Significance of Lattice Enthalpy
subsections.content_text: The Lattice Enthalpy of an ionic solid is defined as the energy required to completely separate one mole of a solid ionic compound into gaseous constituent ions. For example, the lattice enthalpy of $NaCl$ is $788 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$, meaning this much energy is needed to separate one mole of solid $NaCl$ into $Na^+(g)$ and $Cl^-(g)$ to an infinite distance. This value accounts for both attractive forces between opposite charges and repulsive forces between like charges. It is a key factor in determining the stability of ionic crystals.

subsections.title: Bond Length
subsections.content_text: Bond length is defined as the equilibrium distance between the nuclei of two bonded atoms in a molecule. It is measured using spectroscopic, X-ray diffraction, and electron-diffraction techniques. In a covalent bond, each atom contributes to the bond length. The covalent radius is measured as the radius of an atom's core which is in contact with the core of an adjacent atom in a bonded situation, effectively half the distance between two similar atoms joined by a covalent bond.

diagrams.label: Fig. 4.1
diagrams.description: The bond length $R$ in a covalent molecule $AB$ is the sum of the covalent radii $r_A$ and $r_B$ ($R = r_A + r_B$).

subsections.title: Van der Waals Radius
subsections.content_text: The van der Waals radius represents the overall size of the atom, including its valence shell in a nonbonded situation. It is defined as half of the distance between two similar atoms in separate molecules in a solid. The van der Waals radius is always larger than the covalent radius. For example, in chlorine, the covalent radius ($r_c$) is $99 \text{ pm}$, while the van der Waals radius ($r_{vdw}$) is $180 \text{ pm}$.

diagrams.label: Fig. 4.2
diagrams.description: Comparison of covalent and van der Waals radii in a chlorine molecule. Covalent radius $r_c = 99 \text{ pm}$ (half the bond length of $198 \text{ pm}$) and van der Waals radius $r_{vdw} = 180 \text{ pm}$ (half the distance of $360 \text{ pm}$ between non-bonded atoms).

subsections.title: Bond Angle
subsections.content_text: Bond angle is defined as the angle between the orbitals containing bonding electron pairs around the central atom in a molecule or complex ion. It is expressed in degrees and is determined experimentally using spectroscopic methods. Bond angles help in determining the shape of a molecule. For example, the $H-O-H$ bond angle in water is $104.5^\circ$.

subsections.title: Bond Enthalpy
subsections.content_text: Bond enthalpy is defined as the amount of energy required to break one mole of bonds of a particular type between two atoms in a gaseous state. The unit is $\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$. For diatomic molecules, the bond enthalpy is equal to the bond dissociation enthalpy. For example, the $H-H$ bond enthalpy is $435.8 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$. For molecules with multiple bonds, bond enthalpy is higher; for $O_2 (O=O)$ it is $498 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ and for $N_2 (N≡N)$ it is $946.0 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$. In polyatomic molecules, the measurement is more complex as the enthalpy to break successive identical bonds (like the two $O-H$ bonds in $H_2O$) is not the same. In polyatomic molecules, the measurement of bond enthalpy is more complex as the enthalpy to break successive identical bonds is not the same. For example, in water ($H_2O$), the first $O-H$ bond dissociation enthalpy is $502 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$: $$H_2O(g) \rightarrow H(g) + OH(g); \Delta_a H_1^\ominus = 502 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$ The second $O-H$ bond dissociation enthalpy is $427 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$: $$OH(g) \rightarrow H(g) + O(g); \Delta_a H_2^\ominus = 427 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$ The difference in these values shows that the second $O-H$ bond undergoes change due to the altered chemical environment. This also explains why the same bond (like $O-H$) has different energies in different molecules, such as ethanol ($C_2H_5OH$) versus water. In such cases, the term mean or average bond enthalpy is used, obtained by dividing the total bond dissociation enthalpy by the number of bonds broken. For water, the average bond enthalpy is: $$\text{Average bond enthalpy} = \frac{502 + 427}{2} = 464.5 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

subsections.title: 4.3.4 Bond Order
subsections.content_text: In the Lewis description of covalent bonds, the Bond Order is defined as the number of bonds between two atoms in a molecule. For example, the bond order in $H_2$ (one shared electron pair) is 1, in $O_2$ (two shared pairs) is 2, and in $N_2$ (three shared pairs) is 3. Similarly, in $CO$ (three shared pairs), the bond order is 3. For $N_2$, the bond order is 3 and its $\Delta_a H^\ominus$ is $946 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$, which is one of the highest for a diatomic molecule. Isoelectronic molecules and ions (those with the same number of electrons) have identical bond orders; for instance, $F_2$ and $O_2^{2-}$ have bond order 1, while $N_2$, $CO$, and $NO^+$ have bond order 3. A general correlation for understanding molecular stability is that as bond order increases, bond enthalpy increases and bond length decreases.

subsections.title: 4.3.5 Resonance Structures
subsections.content_text: A single Lewis structure is often inadequate to represent a molecule accurately in conformity with its experimentally determined parameters. For example, the ozone ($O_3$) molecule can be represented by two Lewis structures (I and II). In these structures, there is one $O-O$ single bond (normal length 148 pm) and one $O=O$ double bond (normal length 121 pm). However, experimental measurements show that both oxygen-oxygen bond lengths in $O_3$ are identical at 128 pm. This value is intermediate between a single and a double bond, meaning neither structure I nor II accurately depicts the molecule. According to the concept of resonance, whenever a single Lewis structure cannot describe a molecule accurately, a number of structures with similar energy, positions of nuclei, bonding and non-bonding pairs of electrons are taken as the canonical structures (or resonance structures). The actual molecule is described by a resonance hybrid (structure III for $O_3$). Resonance is represented by a double-headed arrow ($\leftrightarrow$).

diagrams.label: Fig. 4.3
diagrams.description: Resonance in the $O_3$ molecule. Structures I and II represent the two canonical forms with alternating single and double bonds (148 pm and 121 pm), while structure III is the resonance hybrid showing equal bond lengths of 128 pm.

subsections.title: Resonance in Carbonate Ion
subsections.content_text: The carbonate ion ($CO_3^{2-}$) provides another example where resonance is necessary. A single Lewis structure showing two single bonds and one double bond between carbon and oxygen atoms is inadequate because experimental evidence shows all three $C-O$ bonds are equivalent. Therefore, the carbonate ion is best described as a resonance hybrid of three canonical forms (I, II, and III).

worked_examples.label: Problem 4.3
worked_examples.problem: Explain the structure of $CO_3^{2-}$ ion in terms of resonance.
worked_examples.solution: The single Lewis structure based on the presence of two single bonds and one double bond between carbon and oxygen atoms is inadequate to represent the molecule accurately as it represents unequal bonds. According to the experimental findings, all carbon to oxygen bonds in $CO_3^{2-}$ are equivalent. Therefore the carbonate ion is best described as a resonance hybrid of the canonical forms I, II, and III.

diagrams.label: Fig. 4.4
diagrams.description: Resonance in $CO_3^{2-}$. Structures I, II, and III represent the three canonical forms where the double bond is distributed among the three oxygen atoms.

subsections.title: Resonance in Carbon Dioxide
subsections.content_text: In the carbon dioxide ($CO_2$) molecule, the experimentally determined $C-O$ bond length is 115 pm. This value lies between the typical lengths of a $C=O$ double bond (121 pm) and a $C \equiv O$ triple bond (110 pm). Consequently, a single Lewis structure cannot depict this position, and $CO_2$ is best described as a hybrid of three canonical resonance forms.

worked_examples.label: Problem 4.4
worked_examples.solution: The experimentally determined carbon to oxygen bond length in $CO_2$ is 115 pm. The lengths of a normal carbon to oxygen double bond ($C=O$) and carbon to oxygen triple bond ($C \equiv O$) are 121 pm and 110 pm respectively. The carbon-oxygen bond lengths in $CO_2$ (115 pm) lie between the values for $C=O$ and $C \equiv O$. Obviously, a single Lewis structure cannot depict this position and it becomes necessary to write more than one Lewis structures and to consider that the structure of $CO_2$ is best described as a hybrid of the canonical or resonance forms I, II and III.

diagrams.label: Fig. 4.5
diagrams.description: Resonance in $CO_2$ molecule. Structures I, II, and III represent the three canonical forms, including structures with triple bonds and formal charges.

subsections.title: Stability and Misconceptions of Resonance
subsections.content_text: Resonance stabilizes the molecule because the energy of the resonance hybrid is less than the energy of any single canonical structure. Resonance also averages the bond characteristics as a whole. It is important to dispel common misconceptions: 1. Canonical forms have no real existence. 2. The molecule does not exist for a fraction of time in one canonical form and for other fractions in others. 3. There is no equilibrium between canonical forms (unlike tautomerism). 4. The molecule exists as a single structure—the resonance hybrid—which cannot be depicted by a single Lewis structure.

subsections.title: 4.3.6 Polarity of Bonds
subsections.content_text: The existence of a 100 percent ionic or covalent bond is an ideal situation; in reality, no bond is purely one or the other. In homonuclear diatomic molecules like $H_2, O_2, Cl_2, N_2,$ or $F_2$, the shared electron pair is equally attracted by the two identical nuclei, resulting in a nonpolar covalent bond. In contrast, in heteronuclear molecules like $HF$, the shared electron pair is displaced more towards the more electronegative atom (Fluorine). This polarization results in a polar covalent bond.

subsections.title: Dipole Moment
subsections.content_text:
As a result of polarization, a molecule possesses a dipole moment ($\mu$), defined as the product of the magnitude of the charge ($Q$) and the distance of separation ($r$) between the centers of positive and negative charge: $$\mu = Q \times r$$ Dipole moment is expressed in Debye units ($D$), where $1 D = 3.33564 \times 10^{-30} \text{ C m}$. It is a vector quantity represented by a crossed arrow ($\mapsto$) placed over the Lewis structure. The cross is on the positive end, and the arrow head points towards the negative end, symbolizing the shift of electron density. This convention is opposite to the conventional direction of the dipole moment vector in physics. The unit is named after Peter Debye, who received the Nobel Prize in 1936 for his work on dipole moments.
In polyatomic molecules, the net dipole moment depends on individual bond dipoles and their spatial arrangement (vector sum). 
- **Water ($H_2O$):** Has a bent structure (angle $104.5^\circ$). The net dipole moment is $1.85 D$ ($6.17 \times 10^{-30} \text{ C m}$), the resultant of two $O-H$ bond dipoles. 
- **Beryllium Fluoride ($BeF_2$):** The net dipole moment is zero because the two equal bond dipoles point in opposite directions and cancel each other. 
- **Boron Trifluoride ($BF_3$):** The net dipole moment is zero. The three $B-F$ bond moments at $120^\circ$ to each other give a net sum of zero. 

Consider the case of $NH_3$ and $NF_3$ molecules. Both have a pyramidal shape with a lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom. Although fluorine is more electronegative than nitrogen, the resultant The resultant dipole moment of $NH_3$ ($4.90 \times 10^{-30}$ C m) is greater than that of $NF_3$ ($0.8 \times 10^{-30}$ C m). This is because, in case of $NH_3$ the orbital dipole due to lone pair is in the same direction as the resultant dipole moment of the three $N-H$ bonds, whereas in $NF_3$ the orbital dipole is in the direction opposite to the resultant dipole moment of the three $N-F$ bonds. The orbital dipole because of lone pair decreases the effect of the resultant $N-F$ bond moments, which results in the low dipole moment of $NF_3$. Dipole moments of some molecules are shown in Table 4.5.

diagrams.label: Dipole Moment in BeF2
diagrams.description: Diagram showing two equal and opposite bond dipoles in the linear BeF2 molecule, resulting in a total dipole moment of zero.

diagrams.label: Dipole Moment in BF3

subsections.title: Fajans' Rules: Partial Covalent Character of Ionic Bonds
subsections.content_text: Just as all covalent bonds have some partial ionic character, ionic bonds also have partial covalent character. The partial covalent character of ionic bonds was discussed by Fajans in terms of the following rules: 1. The smaller the size of the cation and the larger the size of the anion, the greater the covalent character of an ionic bond. 2. The greater the charge on the cation, the greater the covalent character of the ionic bond. 3. For cations of the same size and charge, the one with electronic configuration $(n-1)d^n ns^0$, typical of transition metals, is more polarising than the one with a noble gas configuration, $ns^2 np^6$, typical of alkali and alkaline earth metal cations. The cation polarises the anion, pulling the electronic charge toward itself and thereby increasing the electronic charge between the two. This buildup of electron charge density between the nuclei is precisely what happens in a covalent bond. The polarising power of the cation, the polarisability of the anion, and the extent of distortion (polarisation) of the anion determine the percent covalent character of the ionic bond.

sections.title: THE VALENCE SHELL ELECTRON PAIR REPULSION (VSEPR) THEORY

subsections.title: Introduction to VSEPR Theory
subsections.content_text: The Lewis concept is unable to explain the shapes of molecules. VSEPR theory provides a simple procedure to predict the shapes of covalent molecules. Sidgwick and Powell in 1940 proposed a simple theory based on the repulsive interactions of the electron pairs in the valence shell of the atoms. It was further developed and redefined by Nyholm and Gillespie (1957).

subsections.title: Postulates of VSEPR Theory
subsections.content_text: The main postulates of VSEPR theory are: 1. The shape of a molecule depends upon the number of valence shell electron pairs (bonded or nonbonded) around the central atom. 2. Pairs of electrons in the valence shell repel one another since their electron clouds are negatively charged. 3. These pairs of electrons tend to occupy such positions in space that minimise repulsion and thus maximise distance between them. 4. The valence shell is taken as a sphere with the electron pairs localising on the spherical surface at maximum distance from one another. 5. A multiple bond is treated as if it is a single electron pair and the two or three electron pairs of a multiple bond are treated as a single super pair. 6. Where two or more resonance structures can represent a molecule, the VSEPR model is applicable to any such structure.

subsections.title: Repulsive Interaction of Electron Pairs
subsections.content_text: The repulsive interaction of electron pairs decreases in the order: Lone pair (lp) – Lone pair (lp) > Lone pair (lp) – Bond pair (bp) > Bond pair (bp) – Bond pair (bp). Nyholm and Gillespie (1957) refined the VSEPR model by explaining the important difference between lone pairs and bonding pairs. While lone pairs are localised on the central atom, each bonded pair is shared between two atoms. As a result, lone pair electrons occupy more space as compared to bonding pairs, leading to greater repulsion. These repulsion effects result in deviations from idealised shapes and alterations in bond angles. The theoretical basis regarding the effects of electron pair repulsions on molecular shapes continues to be a subject of discussion.

subsections.title: Classification of Molecules for Geometry Prediction
subsections.content_text: For the prediction of geometrical shapes of molecules with VSEPR theory, it is convenient to divide molecules into two categories: (i) molecules in which the central atom has no lone pair and (ii) molecules in which the central atom has one or more lone pairs. Table 4.6 shows the arrangement of electron pairs about a central atom A (without any lone pairs) and geometries of some molecules/ions of the type $AB$. Table 4.7 (page 115) shows shapes where the central atom has lone pairs, and Table 4.8 (page 116) explains the reasons for distortions.

subsections.title: Geometry of Molecules with No Lone Pairs
subsections.content_text: In compounds of $AB_2, AB_3, AB_4, AB_5$ and $AB_6$, the arrangement of electron pairs and the B atoms around the central atom A are: linear, trigonal planar, tetrahedral, trigonal-bipyramidal and octahedral, respectively. Such arrangements can be seen in molecules like $BF_3$ ($AB_3$), $CH_4$ ($AB_4$) and $PCl_5$ ($AB_5$) as depicted in Fig. 4.6.

diagrams.label: Fig 4.6
diagrams.description: Ball-and-stick models for $BeCl_2$ (linear, 180°), $BF_3$ (trigonal planar, 120°), $CH_4$ (tetrahedral, 109.5°), and $PCl_5$ (trigonal bipyramidal, 90° and 120°).

tables.label: Table 4.6
tables.caption: Geometry of Molecules in which the Central Atom has No Lone Pair of Electrons

subsections.title: Geometry of Molecules with Lone Pairs
subsections.content_text: The presence of one or more lone pairs on the central atom distorts the ideal geometry of a molecule. Table 4.7 summarizes the shapes and geometries of simple molecules or ions where the central atom has lone pairs (represented as E). In these cases, the arrangement of electron pairs (including lone pairs) follows the basic geometries (trigonal planar, tetrahedral, etc.), but the actual molecular shape is determined only by the positions of the atoms. For instance, an $AB_2E$ molecule has a trigonal planar arrangement of electron pairs but a bent molecular shape due to the lone pair.

tables.label: Table 4.7
tables.caption: Shape (geometry) of Some Simple Molecules/Ions with Central Ions having One or More Lone Pairs of Electrons(E).

subsections.title: Detailed Analysis of Shape Distortion due to Lone Pairs
subsections.content_text: The VSEPR theory explains the specific shapes acquired by molecules by considering the relative magnitudes of electron pair repulsions: $lp-lp > lp-bp > bp-bp$. These repulsions cause deviations from ideal bond angles. For example, in $NH_3$, the lone pair-bond pair repulsion is stronger than bond pair-bond pair repulsion, pushing the $N-H$ bonds closer and reducing the angle from $109.5^\circ$ to $107^\circ$. In $H_2O$, with two lone pairs, the $lp-lp$ repulsion is even stronger, reducing the angle further to $104.5^\circ$. For molecules with five electron pairs like $SF_4$ and $ClF_3$, lone pairs preferentially occupy equatorial positions to minimize $90^\circ$ repulsions with bond pairs.

diagrams.label: Fig 4.8.1
diagrams.description: Bent shape of $SO_2$ showing a lone pair on Sulfur and a bond angle of $119.5^\circ$.

diagrams.label: Fig 4.8.2
diagrams.description: Trigonal pyramidal structure of $NH_3$ with one lone pair on Nitrogen and $H-N-H$ bond angles of $107^\circ$.

diagrams.label: Fig 4.8.3
diagrams.description: Bent/Angular structure of $H_2O$ with two lone pairs on Oxygen and an $H-O-H$ bond angle of $104.5^\circ$.

diagrams.label: Fig 4.8.4
diagrams.description: Comparison of lone pair positions in $SF_4$: (a) axial position with three $90^\circ$ $lp-bp$ repulsions, and (b) equatorial position with two $90^\circ$ $lp-bp$ repulsions. Arrangement (b) is more stable, resulting in a see-saw shape.

diagrams.label: Fig 4.8.5
diagrams.description: Comparison of lone pair arrangements in $ClF_3$: (a) both lone pairs in equatorial positions (most stable, T-shape), (b) one axial and one equatorial, and (c) both axial.
