rows: Theoretically the shape should have been triangular planar but actually it is found to be bent or v-shaped. The reason being the lone pair-bond pair repulsion is much more as compared to the bond pair-bond pair repulsion. So the angle is reduced to $119.5^\circ$ from $120^\circ$.

Had there been a bp in place of lp the shape would have been tetrahedral but one lone pair is present and due to the repulsion between lp-bp (which is more than bp-bp repulsion) the angle between bond pairs is reduced to $107^\circ$ from $109.5^\circ$.

The shape should have been tetrahedral if there were all bp but two lp are present so the shape is distorted tetrahedral or angular. The reason is lp-lp repulsion is more than lp-bp repulsion which is more than bp-bp repulsion. Thus, the angle is reduced to $104.5^\circ$ from $109.5^\circ$.

In (a) the lp is present at axial position so there are three lp—bp repulsions at $90^\circ$. In (b) the lp is in an equatorial position, and there are two lp—bp repulsions. Hence, arrangement (b) is more stable. The shape shown in (b) is described as a distorted tetrahedron, a folded square or a see-saw.
In (a) the lp are at equatorial position so there are less lp-bp repulsions as compared to others in which the lp are at axial positions. So structure (a) is most stable. (T-shaped).

subsections.title: Limitations of Lewis and VSEPR Theories
subsections.content_text: While the Lewis approach helps in writing molecular structures, it fails to explain the actual formation of chemical bonds. It does not provide reasons for differences in bond dissociation enthalpies and bond lengths, such as those between $H_2$ ($435.8 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}, 74 \text{ pm}$) and $F_2$ ($155 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}, 144 \text{ pm}$), despite both having single covalent bonds. Furthermore, it offers no insight into the shapes of polyatomic molecules. Similarly, VSEPR theory predicts geometries but lacks a theoretical explanation for them and has limited applications.

subsections.title: Introduction to Valence Bond (VB) Theory
subsections.content_text: To overcome the limitations of earlier models, theories based on quantum mechanical principles were introduced: Valence Bond (VB) theory and Molecular Orbital (MO) theory. VB theory was first introduced by Heitler and London in 1927 and further developed by Pauling and others. It is based on the knowledge of atomic orbitals, electronic configurations of elements, overlap criteria of atomic orbitals, and the principles of variation and superposition.

subsections.title: Formation of the Hydrogen Molecule: Force Analysis
subsections.content_text: Consider the formation of the simplest molecule, $H_2$, from two hydrogen atoms A and B. These atoms have nuclei $N_A, N_B$ and electrons $e_A, e_B$. When the atoms are far apart, there is no interaction. As they approach, new attractive and repulsive forces begin to operate. Attractive forces arise between: (i) the nucleus of one atom and its own electron (i.e., $N_A - e_A$ and $N_B - e_B$). In addition to the forces between a nucleus and its own electron, new attractive forces develop between the nucleus of one atom and the electron of the other ($N_A - e_B$ and $N_B - e_A$). Conversely, repulsive forces arise between the electrons of the two atoms ($e_A - e_B$) and between their nuclei ($N_A - N_B$). Attractive forces act to bring the atoms together, while repulsive forces act to separate them.

diagrams.label: Fig. 4.7
diagrams.description: Illustration of attractive and repulsive forces during the formation of a hydrogen molecule. Attractive forces are shown between the nucleus of one atom and the electron of the other, while repulsive forces are shown between the two electrons and between the two nuclei.

subsections.title: Potential Energy and Bond Enthalpy
subsections.content_text: As two hydrogen atoms approach, the net attractive force exceeds the repulsive force, causing the potential energy of the system to decrease. A state of minimum energy is reached when these forces balance, resulting in a stable $H_2$ molecule with a bond length of 74 pm. The energy released during this formation is the bond enthalpy (435.8 kJ/mol for $H_2$). This same amount of energy is required to dissociate one mole of $H_2$ molecules into individual atoms: $H_2(g) + 435.8 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1} \rightarrow H(g) + H(g)$.

diagrams.label: Fig. 4.8
diagrams.description: A potential energy curve for the formation of an $H_2$ molecule as a function of the internuclear distance. The curve shows a minimum point at a distance of 74 pm, which corresponds to the most stable state and the bond energy of 435.8 kJ/mol.

subsections.title: Orbital Overlap Concept
subsections.content_text: A covalent bond is formed when the atomic orbitals of two atoms partially interpenetrate, a process known as overlapping. This overlap leads to the pairing of electrons with opposite spins in the valence shell. The strength of the resulting covalent bond is determined by the extent of the overlap; generally, a greater overlap results in a stronger bond.

subsections.title: Directional Properties of Bonds
subsections.content_text: Covalent bonds are formed by the overlap of atomic orbitals, which gives them directional character. While this explains simple diatomic molecules like $H_2$, polyatomic molecules such as $CH_4$, $NH_3$, and $H_2$O have specific geometries (tetrahedral, pyramidal, and bent) and bond angles (e.g., $109.5^\circ$ for $CH_4$) that require further explanation through valence bond theory and hybridisation.

subsections.title: Overlapping of Atomic Orbitals: Phase and Orientation
subsections.content_text: The overlap of atomic orbitals can be positive, negative, or zero, depending on the phase (sign) and spatial orientation of the orbital wave functions. Positive or 'in-phase' overlap occurs when the signs of the orbital lobes match, leading to bond formation. Negative or 'out-of-phase' overlap occurs when signs are opposite. Zero overlap occurs when orbitals have different orientations, preventing effective interaction. The criterion of overlap applies to both homonuclear and heteronuclear molecules.

diagrams.label: Fig. 4.9
diagrams.description: Diagrams showing positive, negative, and zero overlaps of s and p atomic orbitals. Positive overlaps include $p_z-p_z$, $p_z-s$, $p_x-p_x$, and $p_y-p_y$ in-phase. Negative overlaps show the same combinations out-of-phase. Zero overlap is shown for $p_x-s$ and $p_x-p_y$ due to perpendicular orientations.

subsections.title: Limitations of Simple Orbital Overlap in Polyatomic Molecules
subsections.content_text: Simple orbital overlap fails to account for the observed bond angles in polyatomic molecules. For methane ($CH_4$), carbon's excited state configuration is $[He] 2s^1 2p_x^1 2p_y^1 2p_z^1$. Overlapping these with hydrogen's 1s orbitals would predict three bonds at $90^\circ$ angles and one bond with no specific direction, which contradicts the experimental tetrahedral geometry with $109.5^\circ$ angles. Similar discrepancies are found in $NH_3$ and $H_2$O, where predicted $90^\circ$ angles differ from the actual $107^\circ$ and $104.5^\circ$ respectively.

subsections.title: Sigma (\sigma) Bond
subsections.content_text: A sigma (\sigma) bond is a type of covalent bond formed by the end-to-end or axial overlap of atomic orbitals along the internuclear axis. This can occur through three types of combinations: s-s overlapping (two s-orbitals), s-p overlapping (one s-orbital and one p-orbital), or p-p overlapping (two p-orbitals meeting head-on).

diagrams.label: Diagram: s-s overlapping
diagrams.description: Two spherical s-orbitals merging along the internuclear axis to form a sigma bond.

diagrams.label: Diagram: s-p overlapping
diagrams.description: A spherical s-orbital and a dumbbell-shaped p-orbital merging along the internuclear axis to form a sigma bond.

diagrams.label: Diagram: p-p overlapping (sigma)
diagrams.description: Two dumbbell-shaped p-orbitals merging head-on along the internuclear axis to form a sigma bond.

subsections.title: Pi (\pi) Bond
subsections.content_text: A pi (\pi) bond is formed by the sidewise or lateral overlap of atomic orbitals such that their axes remain parallel to each other and perpendicular to the internuclear axis. This results in the formation of two saucer-shaped electron clouds, one located above and the other below the plane of the participating atoms.

diagrams.label: Diagram: p-p overlapping (pi)
diagrams.description: Two parallel p-orbitals overlapping sidewise to form a pi bond, showing electron density above and below the internuclear plane.

subsections.title: Strength of Sigma and Pi Bonds
subsections.content_text: The strength of a covalent bond depends on the extent of orbital overlap. Sigma bonds are stronger than pi bonds because axial overlap allows for a greater degree of interpenetration compared to sidewise overlap. In molecules with multiple bonds, a pi bond is always formed in addition to a primary sigma bond.

subsections.title: Concept of Hybridisation
subsections.content_text: To explain the characteristic geometries of polyatomic molecules like $CH_4$, $NH_3$, and $H_2$O, Pauling introduced the concept of hybridisation. It is defined as the process of intermixing atomic orbitals of slightly different energies within an atom to redistribute their energies, resulting in a new set of equivalent orbitals known as hybrid orbitals. For example, one 2s and three 2p orbitals of carbon hybridise to form four equivalent $sp^3$ hybrid orbitals.

subsections.title: Salient Features of Hybridisation
subsections.content_text: The primary features of hybridisation include: 1. The number of hybrid orbitals produced is exactly equal to the number of atomic orbitals that undergo hybridisation. 2. All hybrid orbitals in a set are equivalent in terms of energy and shape. 3. The hybrid orbitals are more effective in forming stable bonds than the pure atomic orbitals. 4. These hybrid orbitals are directed in space in some preferred direction to have minimum repulsion between electron pairs and thus a stable arrangement. Therefore, the type of hybridisation indicates the geometry of the molecules.

subsections.title: Important conditions for hybridisation
subsections.content_text: The following conditions must be met for hybridisation to occur: (i) The orbitals present in the valence shell of the atom are hybridised. (ii) The orbitals undergoing hybridisation should have almost equal energy. (iii) Promotion of electron is not an essential condition prior to hybridisation. (iv) It is not necessary that only half-filled orbitals participate in hybridisation. In some cases, even filled orbitals of the valence shell take part in hybridisation.

subsections.title: sp hybridisation
subsections.content_text:
This type of hybridisation involves the mixing of one $s$ and one $p$ orbital resulting in the formation of two equivalent $sp$ hybrid orbitals. The suitable orbitals for $sp$ hybridisation are $s$ and $p_z$, if the hybrid orbitals are to lie along the $z$-axis. Each $sp$ hybrid orbital has 50% $s$-character and 50% $p$-character. Such a molecule in which the central atom is $sp$-hybridised and linked directly to two other central atoms possesses linear geometry. This type of hybridisation is also known as diagonal hybridisation. The two $sp$ hybrids point in the opposite direction along the $z$-axis with projecting positive lobes and very small negative lobes, which provides more effective overlapping resulting in the formation of stronger bonds.
In $BeCl_2$, the ground state electronic configuration of $Be$ is $1s^2 2s^2$. In the excited state, one of the $2s$-electrons is promoted to a vacant $2p$ orbital to account for its bivalency. One $2s$ and one $2p$-orbital get hybridised to form two $sp$ hybridised orbitals. These two $sp$ hybrid orbitals are oriented in opposite directions forming an angle of $180^\circ$. Each of the $sp$ hybridised orbitals overlaps with the $2p$-orbital of chlorine axially to form two $Be-Cl$ sigma bonds.

diagrams.label: Fig. 4.10 (a)
diagrams.description: Formation of $sp$ hybrids from $s$ and $p$ orbitals showing the combination of an $s$ orbital and a $p_z$ orbital to form two linear $sp$ hybrid orbitals oriented at $180^\circ$.

diagrams.label: Fig. 4.10 (b)
diagrams.description: Formation of the linear $BeCl_2$ molecule showing the overlap of $sp$ hybrid orbitals of Beryllium with the $2p_z$ orbitals of two Chlorine atoms.

subsections.title: sp2 hybridisation
subsections.content_text: In this hybridisation, there is involvement of one $s$ and two $p$-orbitals in order to form three equivalent $sp^2$ hybridised orbitals. For example, in $BCl_3$ molecule, the ground state electronic configuration of the central boron atom is $1s^2 2s^2 2p^1$. In the excited state, one of the $2s$ electrons is promoted to a vacant $2p$ orbital, resulting in boron having three unpaired electrons. These three orbitals (one $2s$ and two $2p$) hybridise to form three $sp^2$ hybrid orbitals. The three hybrid orbitals so formed are oriented in a trigonal planar arrangement and overlap with $2p$ orbitals of chlorine to form three $B-Cl$ bonds. The geometry is trigonal planar with a $Cl-B-Cl$ bond angle of $120^\circ$.

diagrams.label: Fig. 4.11
diagrams.description: Formation of $sp^2$ hybrids from $s, p_x, p_y$ orbitals and the resulting trigonal planar $BCl_3$ molecule with $120^\circ$ angles.

subsections.title: sp3 hybridisation
subsections.content_text: This type of hybridisation involves the mixing of one $s$-orbital and three $p$-orbitals of the valence shell to form four $sp^3$ hybrid orbitals of equivalent energies and shape. There is 25% $s$-character and 75% $p$-character in each $sp^3$ hybrid orbital. The four $sp^3$ hybrid orbitals are directed towards the four corners of a tetrahedron. The angle between $sp^3$ hybrid orbitals is $109.5^\circ$. In $CH_4$, carbon forms four such orbitals that overlap with $1s$ orbitals of hydrogen.

diagrams.label: Fig. 4.12
diagrams.description: Formation of $sp^3$ hybrids by the combination of $s, p_x, p_y$ and $p_z$ atomic orbitals of carbon and the formation of the tetrahedral $CH_4$ molecule.

subsections.title: Structure of NH3 molecule
subsections.content_text: In $NH_3$, the valence shell electronic configuration of nitrogen in the ground state is $2s^2 2p_x^1 2p_y^1 2p_z^1$. It undergoes $sp^3$ hybridisation to form four hybrid orbitals. Three of these contain unpaired electrons which overlap with $1s$ orbitals of hydrogen to form three $N-H$ sigma bonds, while the fourth contains a lone pair. The repulsion between the lone pair and bond pairs is greater than between bond pairs, distorting the molecule and reducing the bond angle from $109.5^\circ$ to $107^\circ$. The geometry is pyramidal.

diagrams.label: Fig. 4.13
diagrams.description: Formation of $NH_3$ molecule showing the $sp^3$ hybrid orbitals of Nitrogen, one containing a lone pair (lp) and three forming bonds with Hydrogen, resulting in a pyramidal shape.

subsections.title: Structure of H2O molecule
subsections.content_text: In the $H_2O$ molecule, the four oxygen orbitals (one $2s$ and three $2p$) undergo $sp^3$ hybridisation forming four $sp^3$ hybrid orbitals. Two of these contain one electron each (forming bonds with Hydrogen) and the other two contain lone pairs. These orbitals acquire a tetrahedral geometry, but with two corners occupied by lone pairs, the bond angle is reduced to $104.5^\circ$. The molecule acquires a V-shape or angular geometry.

diagrams.label: Fig. 4.14
diagrams.description: Formation of $H_2O$ molecule showing the $sp^3$ hybrid orbitals of Oxygen with two lone pairs (lp) and two $O-H$ bonds, resulting in an angular geometry.

sections.title: Other Examples of sp3, sp2 and sp Hybridisation

subsections.title: sp3 Hybridisation in C2H6 molecule
subsections.content_text: In the ethane ($C_2H_6$) molecule, both carbon atoms are in the $sp^3$ hybrid state. One $sp^3$ hybrid orbital from each carbon atom overlaps axially to form a $C-C$ sigma ($\sigma$) bond. The remaining three $sp^3$ hybrid orbitals on each carbon form $sp^3-s$ sigma bonds with hydrogen atoms. The $C-C$ bond length is 154 pm and the $C-H$ bond length is 109 pm.

subsections.title: sp2 Hybridisation in C2H4 molecule
subsections.content_text: In ethene ($C_2H_4$), each carbon atom is $sp^2$ hybridised. One $sp^2$ hybrid orbital from each carbon overlaps axially to form a $C-C$ sigma bond. Two other $sp^2$ orbitals on each carbon form $sp^2-s$ sigma bonds with hydrogen. The unhybridised $2p$ orbital (e.g., $2p_x$ or $2p_y$) of one carbon overlaps sidewise with the corresponding orbital of the other carbon to form a pi ($\pi$) bond. This $\pi$ bond consists of two electron clouds above and below the plane of the molecule. The $C=C$ bond length is 134 pm, $C-H$ is 108 pm, the $H-C-H$ angle is $117.6^\circ$, and the $H-C-C$ angle is $121^\circ$. In the formation of ethyne ($C_2H_2$) molecule, both the carbon atoms undergo $sp$-hybridisation having two unhybridised orbital i.e., $2p_y$ and $2p_x$. One $sp$ hybrid orbital of one carbon atom overlaps axially with $sp$ hybrid orbital of the other carbon atom to form $C-C$ sigma bond, while the other hybridised orbital of each carbon atom overlaps axially with the half filled $s$ orbital of hydrogen atoms forming $\sigma$ bonds. Each of the two unhybridised $p$ orbitals of both the carbon atoms overlaps sidewise to form two $\pi$ bonds between the carbon atoms. So the triple bond between the two carbon atoms is made up of one sigma and two pi bonds as shown in Fig. 4.16.

diagrams.label: Fig. 4.15
diagrams.description: Detailed multi-part diagram showing the formation of sigma and pi bonds in ethene, including the orbital overlaps and the final planar structure with electron clouds above and below the C-C axis.

diagrams.label: Fig. 4.16
diagrams.description: Formation of sigma and pi bonds in ethyne: (a) shows the sigma bond framework formed by axial overlap of sp-sp and sp-1s orbitals; (b) shows the orientation of unhybridised 2px and 2py orbitals; (c) shows the final triple bond structure with two pi electron clouds.

sections.title: Hybridisation of Elements involving d Orbitals

subsections.title: Introduction to d-orbital Hybridisation
subsections.content_text: The elements present in the third period contain $d$ orbitals in addition to $s$ and $p$ orbitals. The energy of the $3d$ orbitals are comparable to the energy of the $3s$ and $3p$ orbitals. The energy of $3d$ orbitals are also comparable to those of $4s$ and $4p$ orbitals. As a consequence the hybridisation involving either $3s, 3p$ and $3d$ or $3d, 4s$ and $4p$ is possible. However, since the difference in energies of $3p$ and $4s$ orbitals is significant, no hybridisation involving $3p, 3d$ and $4s$ orbitals is possible. The important hybridisation schemes involving $s, p$ and $d$ orbitals are summarised in the following table.

tables.label: Table 4.x
tables.caption: Hybridisation schemes involving s, p and d orbitals

subsections.title: Formation of PCl5 (sp3d hybridisation)
subsections.content_text: The ground state and the excited state outer electronic configurations of phosphorus ($Z=15$) involve the promotion of an electron from the $3s$ to a $3d$ orbital. In the excited state, one $s$, three $p$ and one $d$ orbitals are available for hybridisation to yield a set of five $sp^3d$ hybrid orbitals which are directed towards the five corners of a trigonal bipyramidal as depicted in Fig. 4.17. In $PCl_5$, the five $sp^3d$ orbitals of phosphorus overlap with the singly occupied $p$ orbitals of chlorine atoms to form five $P-Cl$ sigma bonds. Three $P-Cl$ bonds lie in one plane and make an angle of $120^\circ$ with each other; these are termed as equatorial bonds. The remaining two $P-Cl$ bonds—one lying above and the other lying below the equatorial plane, make an angle of $90^\circ$ with the plane. These are called axial bonds. As the axial bond pairs suffer more repulsive interaction from the equatorial bond pairs, axial bonds are slightly longer and hence slightly weaker than the equatorial bonds, making $PCl_5$ more reactive.

diagrams.label: Fig. 4.17
diagrams.description: Trigonal bipyramidal geometry of PCl5 molecule showing axial and equatorial P-Cl bonds.

subsections.title: Formation of SF6 (sp3d2 hybridisation)
subsections.content_text: In $SF_6$, the central sulphur atom has the ground state outer electronic configuration $3s^2 3p^4$. In the excited state, the available six orbitals i.e., one $s$, three $p$ and two $d$ are singly occupied by electrons. These orbitals hybridise to form six new $sp^3d^2$ hybrid orbitals, which are projected towards the six corners of a regular octahedron. These six $sp^3d^2$ hybrid orbitals overlap with singly occupied orbitals of fluorine atoms to form six $S-F$ sigma bonds. Thus $SF_6$ molecule has a regular octahedral geometry as shown in Fig. 4.18.

diagrams.label: Fig. 4.18
diagrams.description: Octahedral geometry of SF6 molecule showing the central sulfur atom bonded to six fluorine atoms at the corners of an octahedron.

subsections.title: Salient Features of Molecular Orbital Theory
subsections.content_text: Molecular orbital (MO) theory was developed by F. Hund and R.S. Mulliken in 1932. The salient features are: (i) Electrons in a molecule are present in various molecular orbitals just as electrons in atoms are in atomic orbitals. (ii) Atomic orbitals of comparable energies and proper symmetry combine to form molecular orbitals. (iii) While an electron in an atomic orbital is influenced by one nucleus (monocentric), in a molecular orbital it is influenced by two or more nuclei (polycentric). (iv) The number of molecular orbitals formed is equal to the number of combining atomic orbitals. When two atomic orbitals combine, two molecular orbitals are formed: one bonding molecular orbital and one antibonding molecular orbital. (v) The bonding molecular orbital has lower energy and hence greater stability than the corresponding antibonding molecular orbital. (vi) The electron probability distribution around a group of nuclei in a molecule is given by a molecular orbital. (vii) Molecular orbitals are filled in accordance with the aufbau principle, obeying the Pauli’s exclusion principle and the Hund’s rule.

sections.title: Formation of Molecular Orbitals - Linear Combination of Atomic Orbitals (LCAO)

subsections.title: LCAO Method and Wave Functions
subsections.content_text: According to wave mechanics, atomic orbitals are expressed by wave functions ($\psi$’s) representing electron wave amplitudes, obtained from the Schrödinger equation. Since the equation cannot be solved exactly for multi-electron systems, the Linear Combination of Atomic Orbitals (LCAO) is used as an approximate method. For a homonuclear diatomic hydrogen molecule with atoms A and B, the atomic orbitals are $\psi_A$ and $\psi_B$. The formation of molecular orbitals occurs by addition and subtraction of these wave functions: $\psi_{MO} = \psi_A \pm \psi_B$. This results in two molecular orbitals: the bonding orbital $\sigma = \psi_A + \psi_B$ and the antibonding orbital $\sigma^* = \psi_A - \psi_B$. Qualitatively, bonding orbitals result from constructive interference (reinforcement) of electron waves, while antibonding orbitals result from destructive interference. As a result, the electron density in a bonding molecular orbital is located between the nuclei of the bonded atoms because of which the repulsion between the nuclei is very less while in case of an antibonding molecular orbital, most of the electron density is located away from the space between the nuclei. In fact, there is a nodal plane (on which the electron density is zero) between the nuclei and hence the repulsion between the nuclei is high. Electrons placed in a bonding molecular orbital tend to hold the nuclei together and stabilise a molecule. Therefore, a bonding molecular orbital always possesses lower energy than either of the atomic orbitals that have combined to form it. In contrast, the electrons placed in the antibonding molecular orbital destabilise the molecule. This is because the mutual repulsion of the electrons in this orbital is more than the attraction between the electrons and the nuclei, which causes a net increase in energy. It may be noted that the energy of the antibonding orbital is raised above the energy of the parent atomic orbitals that have combined and the energy of the bonding orbital has been lowered than the parent orbitals. The total energy of two molecular orbitals, however, remains the same as that of two original atomic orbitals.

diagrams.label: Fig. 4.19
diagrams.description: Energy level diagram showing the formation of bonding (sigma) and antibonding (sigma*) molecular orbitals from the linear combination of two atomic orbitals psi_A and psi_B.

sections.title: Conditions for the Combination of Atomic Orbitals

subsections.title: Requirements for LCAO
subsections.content_text: The linear combination of atomic orbitals to form molecular orbitals takes place only if the following conditions are satisfied: 1. The combining atomic orbitals must have the same or nearly the same energy. This means that $1s$ orbital can combine with another $1s$ orbital but not with $2s$ orbital because the energy of $2s$ orbital is appreciably higher than that of $1s$ orbital. This is not true if the atoms are very different. 2. The combining atomic orbitals must have the same symmetry about the molecular axis. By convention z-axis is taken as the molecular axis. It is important to note that atomic orbitals having same or nearly the same energy will not combine if they do not have the same symmetry. For example, $2p_z$ orbital of one atom can combine with $2p_z$ orbital of the other atom but not with the $2p_x$ or $2p_y$ orbitals because of their different symmetries. 3. The combining atomic orbitals must overlap to the maximum extent. Greater the extent of overlap, the greater will be the electron-density between the nuclei of a molecular orbital.

subsections.title: Sigma and Pi Molecular Orbitals
subsections.content_text: Molecular orbitals of diatomic molecules are designated as $\sigma$ (sigma), $\pi$ (pi), $\delta$ (delta), etc. In this nomenclature, the sigma ($\sigma$) molecular orbitals are symmetrical around the bond-axis while pi ($\pi$) molecular orbitals are not symmetrical. For example, the linear combination of $1s$ orbitals centered on two nuclei produces two molecular orbitals which are symmetrical around the bond-axis. Such molecular orbitals are of the $\sigma$ type and are designated as $\sigma 1s$ and $\sigma^* 1s$ [Fig. 4.20(a)]. If internuclear axis is taken to be in the z-direction, it can be seen that a linear combination of $2p_z$ orbitals of two atoms also produces two sigma molecular orbitals designated as $\sigma 2p_z$ and $\sigma^* 2p_z$ [Fig. 4.20(b)]. Molecular orbitals obtained from $2p_x$ and $2p_y$ orbitals are not symmetrical around the bond axis because of the presence of positive lobes above and negative lobes below the molecular plane. Such molecular orbitals, are labelled as $\pi$ and $\pi^*$ [Fig. 4.20(c)]. A $\pi$ bonding MO has larger electron density above and below the inter-nuclear axis. The $\pi^*$ antibonding MO has a node between the nuclei.

sections.title: Energy Level Diagram for Molecular Orbitals

subsections.title: Molecular Orbitals from 2s and 2p Atomic Orbitals
subsections.content_text: We have seen that $1s$ atomic orbitals on two atoms form two molecular orbitals designated as $\sigma 1s$ and $\sigma^* 1s$. In the same manner, the $2s$ and $2p$ atomic orbitals (eight atomic orbitals on two atoms) give rise to the following eight molecular orbitals: Antibonding MOs: $\sigma^* 2s, \sigma^* 2p_z, \pi^* 2p_x, \pi^* 2p_y$; Bonding MOs: $\sigma 2s, \sigma 2p_z, \pi 2p_x, \pi 2p_y$. The energy levels of these molecular orbitals have been determined experimentally from spectroscopic data for homonuclear diatomic molecules of second row elements of the periodic table.

diagrams.label: Fig. 4.20
diagrams.description: Contours and energies of bonding and antibonding molecular orbitals. (a) Two 1s orbitals combine to form bonding $\sigma 1s$ and antibonding $\sigma^* 1s$ orbitals. (b) Two $2p_z$ orbitals combine along the bond axis to form $\sigma 2p_z$ and $\sigma^* 2p_z$. (c) Two $2p_x$ orbitals combine side-on to form $\pi 2p_x$ and $\pi^* 2p_x$ orbitals, showing electron density above and below the internuclear axis and nodal planes in antibonding orbitals.

subsections.title: Energy Order of Molecular Orbitals
subsections.content_text: The increasing order of energies of various molecular orbitals for $O_2$ and $F_2$ is given below: $\sigma 1s < \sigma^* 1s < \sigma 2s < \sigma^* 2s < \sigma 2p_z < (\pi 2p_x = \pi 2p_y) < (\pi^* 2p_x = \pi^* 2p_y) < \sigma^* 2p_z$. However, this sequence of energy levels of molecular orbitals is not correct for the remaining molecules $Li_2, Be_2, B_2, C_2, N_2$. For instance, it has been observed experimentally that for molecules such as $B_2, C_2, N_2$, etc. the increasing order of energies of various molecular orbitals is: $\sigma 1s < \sigma^* 1s < \sigma 2s < \sigma^* 2s < (\pi 2p_x = \pi 2p_y) < \sigma 2p_z < (\pi^* 2p_x = \pi^* 2p_y) < \sigma^* 2p_z$. The important characteristic feature of this order is that the energy of $\sigma 2p_z$ molecular orbital is higher than that of $\pi 2p_x$ and $\pi 2p_y$ molecular orbitals.

subsections.title: Stability of Molecules
subsections.content_text: The distribution of electrons among various molecular orbitals is called the electronic configuration of the molecule. From the electronic configuration of the molecule, it is possible to get important information about the molecule as discussed below: Stability of Molecules: If $N_b$ is the number of electrons occupying bonding orbitals and $N_a$ the number occupying the antibonding orbitals, then (i) the molecule is stable if $N_b$ is greater than $N_a$, and (ii) the molecule is unstable if $N_b$ is less than $N_a$. In (i) more bonding orbitals are occupied and so the bonding influence is stronger and a stable molecule results. In (ii) the antibonding influence is stronger and therefore the molecule is unstable.

subsections.title: Bond Order
subsections.content_text: Bond order (b.o.) is defined as one half the difference between the number of electrons present in the bonding and the antibonding orbitals i.e., Bond order (b.o.) = $\frac{1}{2}(N_b - N_a)$. The rules discussed above regarding the stability of the molecule can be restated in terms of bond order as follows: A positive bond order (i.e., $N_b > N_a$) means a stable molecule while a negative (i.e., $N_b < N_a$) or zero (i.e., $N_b = N_a$) bond order means an unstable molecule.

subsections.title: Nature, Length, and Magnetic Properties
subsections.content_text: Nature of the bond: Integral bond order values of 1, 2 or 3 correspond to single, double or triple bonds respectively as studied in the classical concept. Bond-length: The bond order between two atoms in a molecule may be taken as an approximate measure of the bond length. The bond length decreases as bond order increases. Magnetic nature: If all the molecular orbitals in a molecule are doubly occupied, the substance is diamagnetic (repelled by magnetic field). However if one or more molecular orbitals are singly occupied it is paramagnetic (attracted by magnetic field), e.g., $O_2$ molecule.

sections.title: BONDING IN SOME HOMONUCLEAR DIATOMIC MOLECULES

subsections.title: Hydrogen Molecule (H2)
subsections.content_text: In this section we shall discuss bonding in some homonuclear diatomic molecules. 1. Hydrogen molecule ($H_2$): It is formed by the combination of two hydrogen atoms. Each hydrogen atom has one electron in $1s$ orbital. Therefore, in all there are two electrons in hydrogen molecule which are present in $\sigma 1s$ molecular orbital. So electronic configuration of hydrogen molecule is $H_2 : (\sigma 1s)^2$. The bond order of $H_2$ molecule can be calculated as given below: Bond order = $\frac{N_b - N_a}{2} = \frac{2 - 0}{2} = 1$. This means that the two hydrogen atoms are bonded together by a single covalent bond. The bond dissociation energy of hydrogen molecule has been found to be 438 kJ mol$^{-1}$ and bond length equal to 74 pm. Since no Since no unpaired electron is present in hydrogen molecule, therefore, it is diamagnetic.

subsections.title: Helium molecule (He2)
subsections.content_text: The electronic configuration of helium atom is $1s^2$. Each helium atom contains 2 electrons, therefore, in $He_2$ molecule there would be 4 electrons. These electrons will be accommodated in $\sigma 1s$ and $\sigma^* 1s$ molecular orbitals leading to electronic configuration: $He_2 : (\sigma 1s)^2 (\sigma^* 1s)^2$. The bond order of $He_2$ is $\frac{1}{2}(2 - 2) = 0$. $He_2$ molecule is therefore unstable and does not exist. Similarly, it can be shown that $Be_2$ molecule with configuration $(\sigma 1s)^2 (\sigma^* 1s)^2 (\sigma 2s)^2 (\sigma^* 2s)^2$ also does not exist.

subsections.title: Lithium molecule (Li2)
subsections.content_text: The electronic configuration of lithium is $1s^2 2s^1$. There are six electrons in $Li_2$. The electronic configuration of $Li_2$ molecule, therefore, is $Li_2 : (\sigma 1s)^2 (\sigma^* 1s)^2 (\sigma 2s)^2$. The above configuration is also written as $KK(\sigma 2s)^2$ where $KK$ represents the closed K shell structure $(\sigma 1s)^2 (\sigma^* 1s)^2$. From the electronic configuration of $Li_2$ molecule it is clear that there are four electrons present in bonding molecular orbitals and two electrons present in antibonding molecular orbitals. Its bond order, therefore, is $\frac{1}{2}(4 - 2) = 1$. It means that $Li_2$ molecule is stable and since it has no unpaired electrons it should be diamagnetic. Indeed diamagnetic $Li_2$ molecules are known to exist in the vapour phase.

subsections.title: Carbon molecule (C2)
subsections.content_text: The electronic configuration of carbon is $1s^2 2s^2 2p^2$. There are twelve electrons in $C_2$. The electronic configuration of $C_2$ molecule, therefore, is $C_2 : (\sigma 1s)^2 (\sigma^* 1s)^2 (\sigma 2s)^2 (\sigma^* 2s)^2 (\pi 2p_x^2 = \pi 2p_y^2)$ or $KK (\sigma 2s)^2 (\sigma^* 2s)^2 (\pi 2p_x^2 = \pi 2p_y^2)$. The bond order of $C_2$ is $\frac{1}{2}(8 - 4) = 2$ and $C_2$ should be diamagnetic. Diamagnetic $C_2$ molecules have indeed been detected in vapour phase. It is important to note that double bond in $C_2$ consists of both pi bonds because of the presence of four electrons in two pi molecular orbitals. In most of the other molecules a double bond is made up of a sigma bond and a pi bond. In a similar fashion the bonding in $N_2$ molecule can be discussed.

subsections.title: Oxygen molecule (O2)
subsections.content_text: The electronic configuration of oxygen atom is $1s^2 2s^2 2p^4$. Each oxygen atom has 8 electrons, hence, in $O_2$ molecule there are 16 electrons. The electronic configuration of $O_2$ molecule, therefore, is $O_2 : (\sigma 1s)^2 (\sigma^* 1s)^2 (\sigma 2s)^2 (\sigma^* 2s)^2 (\sigma 2p_z)^2 (\pi 2p_x^2 = \pi 2p_y^2) (\pi^* 2p_x^1 = \pi^* 2p_y^1)$. From the electronic configuration of $O_2$ molecule it is clear that ten electrons are present in bonding molecular orbitals and six electrons are present in antibonding molecular orbitals. Its bond order, therefore, is $\text{Bond order} = \frac{1}{2} [N_b - N_a] = \frac{1}{2} [10 - 6] = 2$. So in oxygen molecule, atoms are held by a double bond. Moreover, it may be noted that it contains two unpaired electrons in $\pi^* 2p_x$ and $\pi^* 2p_y$ molecular orbitals, therefore, $O_2$ molecule should be paramagnetic, a prediction that corresponds to experimental observation. In this way, the theory successfully explains the paramagnetic nature of oxygen.

subsections.title: Molecular Orbital Occupancy and Properties for Second Row Diatomic Molecules
subsections.content_text: The electronic configurations of other homonuclear diatomic molecules of the second row of the periodic table can be written similarly. Fig. 4.21 provides the molecular orbital occupancy and molecular properties for $B_2$ through $Ne_2$. The sequence of MOs and their electron population are shown. The bond energy, bond length, bond order, magnetic properties and valence electron configuration appear below the orbital diagrams. Note that for $B_2, C_2,$ and $N_2$, there is 2s-2p mixing which changes the energy order of $\sigma 2p_z$ and $\pi 2p$ orbitals, whereas for $O_2, F_2,$ and $Ne_2$, there is no such mixing.

diagrams.label: Fig. 4.21
diagrams.description: Molecular orbital occupancy and molecular properties for $B_2$ through $Ne_2$. The diagram shows energy levels for molecular orbitals, highlighting the difference in energy ordering between molecules with 2s-2p mixing ($B_2, C_2, N_2$) and those without ($O_2, F_2, Ne_2$). Below the energy levels, bar charts represent bond energy (kJ/mol) and bond length (pm), followed by a table of bond order, magnetic properties, and valence electron configurations.

subsections.title: Definition of Hydrogen Bonding
subsections.content_text: Nitrogen, oxygen and fluorine are highly electronegative elements. When they are attached to a hydrogen atom to form a covalent bond, the electrons of the covalent bond are shifted towards the more electronegative atom. This partially positively charged hydrogen atom forms a bond with another more electronegative atom. This bond is known as a hydrogen bond and is weaker than a covalent bond. For example, in HF molecule, the hydrogen bond exists between the hydrogen atom of one molecule and the fluorine atom of another molecule: $--- H^{\delta+} - F^{\delta-} --- H^{\delta+} - F^{\delta-} --- H^{\delta+} - F^{\delta-}$. Here, the hydrogen bond acts as a bridge between two atoms, holding one by a covalent bond and the other by a hydrogen bond. It is represented by a dotted line ($---$) while a solid line represents the covalent bond. Thus, hydrogen bond can be defined as the attractive force which binds a hydrogen atom of one molecule with the electronegative atom (F, O or N) of another molecule.

subsections.title: Cause of Formation of Hydrogen Bond
subsections.content_text: When hydrogen is bonded to a strongly electronegative element 'X', the electron pair shared between the two atoms moves far away from the hydrogen atom. As a result, the hydrogen atom becomes highly electropositive with respect to the other atom 'X'. Since there is a displacement of electrons towards X, the hydrogen acquires a fractional positive charge ($\delta^+$) while 'X' attains a fractional negative charge ($\delta^-$). This results in the formation of a polar molecule having an electrostatic force of attraction which can be represented as: $H^{\delta+} - X^{\delta-} --- H^{\delta+} - X^{\delta-} --- H^{\delta+} - X^{\delta-}$. The magnitude of H-bonding depends on the physical state of the compound; it is maximum in the solid state and minimum in the gaseous state. Thus, hydrogen bonds have a strong influence on the structure and properties of compounds.

subsections.title: Types of H-Bonds
subsections.content_text: There are two types of H-bonds: (i) Intermolecular hydrogen bond and (ii) Intramolecular hydrogen bond. (1) Intermolecular hydrogen bond: It is formed between two different molecules of the same or different compounds. Examples include H-bonds in HF, alcohol, or water molecules. (2) Intramolecular hydrogen bond: It is formed when a hydrogen atom is in between two highly electronegative (F, O, N) atoms present within the same molecule. For example, in o-nitrophenol, the hydrogen is between the two oxygen atoms.

diagrams.label: Fig. 4.22
diagrams.description: Intramolecular hydrogen bonding in an o-nitrophenol molecule, showing the hydrogen atom forming a bridge between the oxygen of the hydroxyl group and an oxygen of the nitro group within the same molecule.

sections.title: Summary of Chemical Bonding and Molecular Structure

subsections.title: Core Concepts of Bonding
subsections.content_text: Kössel’s first insight into the mechanism of formation of electropositive and electronegative ions related the process to the attainment of noble gas configurations by the respective ions. Electrostatic attraction between ions is the cause for their stability, giving the concept of electrovalency. The first description of covalent bonding was provided by Lewis in terms of the sharing of electron pairs between atoms to attain noble gas configurations. Lewis dot symbols show valence electrons, and Lewis dot structures show pictorial representations of bonding. An ionic compound is a three-dimensional aggregation of ions in a crystal lattice, stabilized by the enthalpy of lattice formation. Covalent bonds can be single or multiple (double/triple). Lone-pairs are valence electrons not involved in bonding. Important parameters include bond length, bond angle, bond enthalpy, bond order, and bond polarity. Resonance occurs when a single Lewis structure cannot accurately describe a molecule; the resonance hybrid represents the actual structure. The VSEPR model is a tool for predicting molecular geometry based on the repulsion between electron pairs. It posits that electron pairs around a central atom arrange themselves as far apart as possible to minimize repulsion. The strength of these repulsions follows a specific hierarchy: lone pair-lone pair interactions are the strongest, followed by lone pair-bond pair, and finally bond pair-bond pair interactions.

subsections.title: Valence Bond Theory
subsections.content_text: Valence Bond (VB) theory explains covalent bonding through the lens of atomic orbital overlap and system energetics. It describes how the potential energy of a system decreases as two atoms approach each other, reaching a minimum at the bond length. If atoms are forced closer than this equilibrium distance, repulsive forces dominate, causing a sharp energy increase and destabilizing the bond.

subsections.title: Hybridisation
subsections.content_text: Pauling introduced hybridisation to account for the observed shapes of polyatomic molecules. This process involves the mathematical mixing of atomic orbitals to create new, equivalent hybrid orbitals like $sp$, $sp^2$, and $sp^3$. This concept explains the geometry of simple molecules like methane and water, as well as the formation of multiple bonds in unsaturated hydrocarbons.

subsections.title: Molecular Orbital Theory
subsections.content_text: Molecular Orbital (MO) theory views bonding as the combination of atomic orbitals to form molecular orbitals that encompass the entire molecule. The number of resulting MOs equals the number of initial atomic orbitals. Bonding MOs concentrate electron density between nuclei and are lower in energy, while antibonding MOs have a nodal region between nuclei and are higher in energy.

subsections.title: Molecular Electronic Configuration
subsections.content_text: Electrons are assigned to molecular orbitals in order of increasing energy, following the Pauli exclusion principle and Hund's rule. The stability of a molecule is determined by the relative number of electrons in bonding ($N_b$) versus antibonding ($N_a$) orbitals; a molecule is stable only if $N_b > N_a$.

subsections.title: Hydrogen Bonding
subsections.content_text: Hydrogen bonding occurs when a hydrogen atom is covalently bonded to a highly electronegative atom (like F, O, or N) and experiences an attraction to another electronegative atom. These bonds can occur between different molecules (intermolecular) or within a single molecule (intramolecular), significantly impacting the physical properties of the substance.
